claimed_contribution: Introduces anticipation of future variety as a new psychological determinant that can delay satiation during ongoing consumption.

Identifies a cognitive mechanism in which anticipating future variety generates positive anticipatory thoughts, and documents boundary conditions based on category relatedness and relative attractiveness.
Distinguishes anticipating future variety from recalling past variety and contributes to anticipation utility by examining anticipation of a different (not necessarily better) future outcome.
context: The paper studies repeated consumption in controlled lab settings with U.S. university students. The empirical contexts include eating jelly beans (food) and listening repeatedly to music clips (nonfood), while varying what participants expect to consume in the future.
design_and_data: The authors run five experiments (Study 1 N=185, Study 2 N=322, Study 3 N=148, Study 4 N=292, Study 5 N=330). Studies 1-4 use repeated jelly-bean consumption tasks with between-subject manipulations of future consumption (same vs different flavor, different category, and better vs worse variety), plus control and past-variety recall conditions in some studies; Study 5 uses repeated music-listening episodes. Satiation is assessed using enjoyment decline over repetitions, end-of-task enjoyment, desire to consume more, willingness to pay, and retrospective satisfaction/enjoyment. Mechanism tests use measured thoughts about future consumption and an induced positive-vs-negative anticipatory-thoughts moderation design.
main_findings: Anticipating future variety within the same category reduced satiation from current repeated consumption relative to anticipating the same future outcome or having no future episode.

The effect was mediated by thinking about the future consumption experience, and when anticipatory thoughts were experimentally induced to be positive or negative, the baseline future-variety effect disappeared.

The effect had boundary conditions: it did not reduce satiation when future variety was in a different category, and it weakened or reversed when the future outcome was less attractive; recalling past variety did not produce the same reduction in current satiation.
one_line_summary: Across five experiments, expecting to consume a different future option slowed satiation in current repeated consumption via anticipatory thoughts, but only when the future option was related and not less attractive.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior satiation and anticipation-utility research by showing that anticipation of a different future outcome can change the current satiation trajectory, and by separating this process from past-variety recall effects.
research_gap: Prior satiation work had not examined whether anticipating future variety changes satiation during current consumption. Prior anticipation-utility research had not tested anticipation of a merely different (rather than better or worse) future outcome as a driver of current satiation rate.
research_question: Does anticipating a varied future consumption experience reduce the rate of satiation from repeated current consumption? If so, is the effect driven by anticipatory thoughts about that future experience, and when does it hold based on product-category relatedness and the future outcome's attractiveness?
